persons.arguments_against: The strongest case against: with 50 Bowens in the staff lists, a relative named George Ferguson Bowen could have held one of these governorships, or the stints could be a successor coincidence. This fails comprehensively. Both stints print the full distinctive double forename 'Sir George Ferguson Bowen' with 'G.C.M.G.' — the bio's exact honour ('G.C.M.G. in 1860') — in the exact colonies and years the biography claims: 'governor of Mauritius, 1879' (stint listed 1879-1880, 'Governor and Commander-in-Chief') and 'of Hong Kong, 1883' (stint listed 1883-1886, 'Governor'). Governorships are unique single-occupant posts; two contemporaneous Sir George Ferguson Bowens G.C.M.G. is not a live hypothesis. The successor hypothesis fails because the names and honours match verbatim, not just the office. Chronology coheres as one career: Victoria to 1879, Mauritius 1879-83, Hong Kong 1883, retired 1887.
persons.would_overturn: Documentary evidence of a second Sir George Ferguson Bowen, G.C.M.G., governing Mauritius or Hong Kong in these years — effectively impossible for unique vice-regal posts with full-name and honour agreement.

notes: Full double-forename match, unique gubernatorial offices, exact years, and shared G.C.M.G. on both stints. The lack of edition co-occurrence is expected: the staff lists record him in 1879/1883 editions while the biographical section entries run 1886-1899.
